Savage Spite
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AMELA FAKO FOUND ONLY ONE FAMILY PHOTO when she returned to her home in the Sarajevo suburb of Ilidza last month. The picture, taken in the 1970's in front  of the Hotel Serbia in Ilidza, showed her with her family; now, their faces were obliterated by a drill bit. "The picture was a primitive message, part of the other war, one not done with guns," Fako said. "It was the war of fear, the war  on the mind. They wanted to make it difficult for us to come home." 

     The rest of the house had been cleaned out. All of the Fako family's possessions and nearly all of the house's fixtures -- windows, doors, bathroom tiles and even the wood on the floor -- had been removed by the Bosnian Serb nationalists who had squatted there for the last four years. It was their way of compensating for the impending loss of what had become theirs through conquest.

WHAT'S DOING IN;Vancouver
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The new International Terminal at Vancouver Airport, which will open May 1, epitomizes the city's 10-year tourist boom that began during Expo '86. Since that world's fair, Vancouver has been transformed from a pleasant metropolitan area with the charms of a small town to a sophisticated, vibrant city dotted with innovative architecture. 

     What remains unchanged is its glorious setting: peninsulas flanked on the north by the soaring snowcapped peaks of the Coastal Range. Stanley Park, a 1,000-acre forested haven, still dominates the tip of downtown. Even amid glassy downtown high-rises, it is generally possible to glimpse patches of rocky mountain slopes or slivers of sea and passing ships.

A Man From Radio Land
nytimes_articles.abstract: Media Marathon A Twentieth-Century Memoir. By Erik Barnouw. Illustrated. 264 pp. Durham, N.C.: Duke University Press. $22.95.     MOST of us know Erik Barnouw as a professor of mass media or as the author of books on cinema and of a magisterial three-volume "History of Broadcasting in the United States," still definitive after almost 30 years. "Since the dawn of history," it begins, "each new medium has tended to undermine an old monopoly, shift the definitions of goodness and greatness, and alter the climate of men's  lives." They certainly altered his life: Mr. Barnouw's soft-spoken, enjoyable memoir, shaped as a series of character sketches of interesting and distinguished people he has encountered, reveals a "white-collar vagabond" career that has lurched from theater to radio to teaching to television and film.

VIEWPOINT;A Handshake Unwise for Europe
nytimes_articles.abstract:
NOTWITHSTANDING negative forecasts, European monetary unification is likely to happen on schedule on Jan. 1, 1999. But while there are clear potential economic benefits, unification is being carried out in a way that is likely to produce unfortunate effects for Europe and its trading partners, including the United States. 

     Already, the move toward unification has slowed growth and increased unemployment in Europe.  Now, France and Germany have agreed on a structure that will impede Europe's ability to respond effectively to economic downturns.

Books in Brief: NONFICTION;The Art of Disintegration
nytimes_articles.abstract: Something has decomposed, and it is from that decomposition that hope comes  again," the artist Louise Bourgeois says of her not-quite-abstract image of three maggots -- ovoids formed by bands of white pastel and blood-red gouache streaks on black paper. The artworks in LOUISE BOURGEOIS: Drawings & Observations (University Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive/Bulfinch/Little, Brown, $24.95) prove her point, demonstrating the possibilities of drawing today, after its "death" as a mandatory artistic skill. In contrast to Ms. Bourgeois's elegant, highly finished sculptures, her drawings are impromptu records of raw feeling registered in symbolic form. The artist's absorbing commentary (recorded during interviews with Lawrence Rinder, curator for 20th-century art at the University Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive at the University of California, Berkeley, who also contributes an excellent introduction) reveals the emotional and philosophical roots of her imagery. With an acute sensitivity to materials and form, she fuses elusive iconography with subtly inviting colors and textures. In these charged graphic works, the act of  drawing weds decomposition and composition, providing release and "hope" -- for  Louise Bourgeois, and for us.   Siri Huntoon

Eating Disorders Strike Younger Girls and Men
nytimes_articles.abstract:
EATING disorders on Long Island are increasing, as well as crossing age, sex  and class lines, experts in the field say. 

     Although often associated with middle- and upper-class teen-age girls, anorexia nervosa is now being reported more frequently in pre-adolescent girls,  adolescent boys and middle-age women on Long Island, and, some experts say, nationally. It is also crossing class lines. Bulimia, which affects a broad range of groups, is also reported to be increasing. An estimated seven million women and one million men nationally suffer from eating disorders, said Chris Aisas, a spokesman for the National Association for Anorexia Nervosa and Assorted Disorders in Highland Park, Ill.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BROOKLYN HEIGHTS/DOWNTOWN BROOKLYN;Things Get Messier in Tug of War at Church of St. Ann
nytimes_articles.abstract:
While the Episcopal Church of St. Ann and the Holy Trinity, an architectural  treasure on Montague Street, continues to fall into disrepair, the bishop, the rector and the vestry remain locked in a struggle that has paralyzed the church. 

     The new rector has all but disappeared, suspending all her functions at the Gothic Revival church. The Episcopal Diocese of Long Island, which covers Brooklyn, has obtained a restraining order and an injunction against the vestry, the 14-member governing body elected by the parishioners, blocking it from any involvement in church matters. And the vestry has refused to give up its powers, waiting for the matter to be settled in court.

JERSEYANA;Just an Old-Fashioned Town, Filled With Music, Art and Fish This Weekend
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Fish and people are pouring into Lambertville and its Pennsylvania neighbor New Hope this weekend for the 15th annual Shad Fest, a popular showcase not only for art and music, but also for the town of Lambertville itself. 

     In its heyday, the town, a ferry crossing that became a manufacturing and transportation hub in the 19th century, saw the construction of picturesque churches, stately commercial buildings, and elegant Second Empire and Victorian  houses. As economic development moved south, recessions and Lambertville's physical isolation in the Delaware Valley had the beneficial effect of causing the village to enter the mid-20th century largely undeveloped and unchanged.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
DOG Bite Sunday was the day I realized it was unsafe to hang out on my roof anymore. I resent giving up that pleasure. 

     During warm weather, I used to take coffee breaks there. I'd sit outside for  20 minutes, enjoy the sun and rest my eyes from the computer. If I was expecting a call, I'd bring up the cordless. Breaking on the roof was convenient and fun. Now I'm forced to leave my building if I want fresh air.

Acres and Acres of Color
nytimes_articles.abstract:
POISED where the piedmont meets the coastal plains, the pine-forested mountains of west central Georgia, formed from resilient Hollis quartzite, are scored by dramatic ravines and streaked with pink and white dogwoods in the spring; nearby farmlands, once fleecy with cotton, now are host to the full range of botanical specimens from the southeastern United States and an ever-blooming palette. This swatch of land is sometimes called the Chattahoochee Trace after the river that wanders down from the state's northern  mountains to form the border with Alabama. It embraces 200 miles of scenic country roads and some of Georgia's most carefully preserved landscape and history -- all because of a rare native plant called the plumleaf azalea and a hot springs that simmers at 88 degrees year round. 

     In the 1930's, Cason Callaway, a textile magnate from nearby La Grange, and his wife, Virginia, were exploring land they had purchased near Blue Springs at  Pine Mountain, a steeply ridged formation that makes up the last spine of the Appalachian range.  When the two discovered a brilliant reddish orange azalea, Virginia identified it as the prunifloria, or plumleaf, variety that grows only  within a hundred-mile radius of the spot, and Callaway vowed to build a magnificent garden to reclaim the region's worn-out cotton fields and preserve its flora for the public to enjoy.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
Myth expresses in a concise and symbolic way some truth of human experience,  possibly even some fact. Nico, a German model who became the lead singer of the  Velvet Underground and died eight years ago of a brain hemorrhage, was a particular myth of the downtown, bohemian, Andy-Warhol-factory, cool-beyond-cool 1960's. As it turns out, however, Nico is not very mythic at all. The idea of her may be interesting, but she is barely an instrument of expression. She has the blankness, the emptiness of a mirror. 

     In the documentary "Nico Icon," made by Susanne Ofteringer, several people remark with some awe and little irony that Nico was careless of her beauty, that she was proud of her bad teeth and the needle marks on her arms. This is odd, because she is still very beautiful, even with the decaying teeth of a junkie. What is even odder is the notion that Nico's supposed disregard of self is an example of high spirits, a sectarian dissidence as opposed to a vanity so encompassing that her own destruction becomes boast. Her detachment constitutes  for her an icy neutrality. Her refusal even to be interested in the resolution of her own story causes her to appear deceptively egoless. It is not as if she were living a life, as desperate and desperately glamorous as it may have appeared -- money, drugs, boyfriends like Jim Morrison and Alain Delon, straight blond hair -- so much as passing the time.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;A Widow's Walk On the Wild Side
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A WIDOW and her money are soon parted -- unless she is careful and wise. I am not talking about a street scam or a swindle. Most of us are too cool for that.  There are other ways, and I have tried them all. 

     Almost every suburban widow I know, myself included, buys a new car as soon as the estate is settled. I don't know the psychology behind it, but once alone  we feel a need for something bigger, safer, newer. A side benefit is that the search for the right vehicle diverts the mind and energy as we bridge the gap between marriage and widowhood. It is the first indication that the bereaved have stopped looking over their shoulders and are going forward -- in an eight-cylinder tank.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: HARLEM;Apollo Bronze Gone
nytimes_articles.abstract: Perhaps it was a crazed souvenir hunter. Or perhaps a scavenger eager to deal 100 pounds of bronze. Whoever it was, officials at the Apollo Theater are outraged at the theft of the 30-by-42 inch plaque proclaiming the theater a Federal, state and city landmark. The plaque had been bolted to the wall at the  West 125th Street entrance. "I cannot understand why anyone would want to hurt us in this way," said Grace Blake, executive director of the Apollo Theater Foundation.   SHARON CHETTY

IF you've ever refinanced a mortgage, no doubt you wished that the lower rate had been available when you bought the house -- that the bank would roll back the date of the new rate and return the extra cash, or perhaps give you a year's grace on your house payments. 

     Sound ridiculous? For you, it is. But for the State of New Jersey, it's a scenario not unlike last month's settlement with the Internal Revenue Service.

wiki_events.headline: Whitewater controversy: President Bill Clinton gives a 41⁄2 hour videotaped testimony for the defense.

pages.title: Whitewater controversy
pages.abstract: The Whitewater controversy, Whitewater scandal, or simply Whitewater, was an American political controversy during the 1990s, surrounding the Whitewater Development Corporation, a real estate company owned by President Bill Clinton and First Lady Hillary Clinton and their associates, Jim and Susan McDougal.

pages.title: Bill Clinton
pages.description: President of the United States from 1993 to 2001
pages.abstract: William Jefferson Clinton is an American politician and lawyer who served as the 42nd president of the United States from 1993 to 2001. A member of the Democratic Party, he previously served as the attorney general of Arkansas from 1977 to 1979 and as the governor of Arkansas from 1979 to 1981, and again from 1983 to 1992. His centrist "Third Way" political philosophy became known as Clintonism, which dominated his presidency and the succeeding decades of Democratic Party history.

wiki_events.headline: Port Arthur massacre, Tasmania: A gunman, Martin Bryant, opens fire at the Broad Arrow Cafe in Port Arthur, Tasmania, killing 35 people and wounding 23 others.

pages.title: Port Arthur massacre
pages.abstract: The Port Arthur massacre was a mass shooting that occurred on 28 April 1996 at Port Arthur, a tourist town in the Australian state of Tasmania. The perpetrator, Martin Bryant, killed 35 people and wounded 23 others, in the deadliest massacre in modern Australian history. The attack led to fundamental changes in Australia's gun laws.

pages.title: Martin Bryant
pages.abstract: Martin John Bryant is an Australian mass murderer who shot and killed 35 people and injured 23 others in the Port Arthur massacre on 28 and 29 April 1996. He is currently serving 35 life sentences, and 1,652 years without the possibility of parole, at Risdon Prison in Hobart, Tasmania.

pages.title: Port Arthur, Tasmania
pages.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site in Australia
pages.abstract: Port Arthur is a town and former convict settlement on the Tasman Peninsula, in Tasmania, Australia. It is located approximately 97 kilometres (60 mi) southeast of the state capital, Hobart.

Regents' Plan Will Hurt Advanced Students
Up From Affirmative Action
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The debate over affirmative action gained momentum last week when the United  States Supreme Court refused to block a Mississippi mandate that all of the state's universities, including its three traditionally black institutions, must have the same admission standards. Coming on the heels of a Federal appeals court ruling against race-based preferences at the University of Texas Law School, the decision has led some to fear that blacks will be shut out of higher education. 

     The critics are right only insofar as they say our nation has failed to build a color-blind society. The problem is that rather than equalizing playing fields, we have created government laws and programs that promote state-sponsored discrimination in the form of affirmative action. We try to convince ourselves that targeted discrimination is appropriate. It is not.
